metadata.author: John of Damascus: Exposition of the Orthodox Faith

ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning the reason of our endowment with Free-will.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: We hold, therefore, that free-will comes on the scene at the same moment as reason, and that change and alteration are congenital to all that is produced.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For all that is produced is also subject to change.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For those things must be subject to change whose production has its origin in change.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And change consists in being brought into being out of nothing, and in transforming a substratum of matter into something different.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Inanimate things, then, and things without reason undergo the aforementioned bodily changes, while the changes of things endowed with reason depend on choice.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For reason consists of a speculative and a practical part.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The speculative part is the contemplation of the nature of things, and the practical consists in deliberation and defines the true reason for what is to be done.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The speculative side is called mind or wisdom, and the practical side is called reason or prudence.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Every one, then, who deliberates does so in the belief that the choice of what is to be done lies in his hands, that he may choose what seems best as the result of his deliberation, and having chosen may act upon it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And if this is so, free-will must necessarily be very closely related to reason.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For either man is an irrational being, or, if he is rational, he is master of his acts and endowed with free-will.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Hence also creatures without reason do not enjoy free-will: for nature leads them rather than they nature, and so they do not oppose the natural appetite, but as soon as their appetite longs after anything they rush headlong after it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But man, being rational, leads nature rather than nature him, and so when he desires aught he has the power to curb his appetite or to indulge it as he pleases.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Hence also creatures devoid of reason are the subjects neither of praise nor blame, while man is the subject of both praise and blame.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Note also that the angels, being rational, are endowed with free-will, and, inasmuch as they are created, are liable to change.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: This in fact is made plain by the devil who, although made good by the Creator, became of his own free-will the inventor of evil, and by the powers who revolted with him, that is the demons, and by the other troops of angels who abode in goodness.
